coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots doping SnO2 for improving carrier transport of perovskite solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Chaoqun
Zhang, Weijia
Jiang, Zhaoyi
Zhang, Yulong
Ni, Cong
coredata.dc:description: SnO2 is recognized as an excellent electron transport layer. However, it does not possess the optimal morphology required by perovskite photovoltaic devices, and the conduction band of SnO2 cannot match well with perovskite conduction bands. In the present study, we introduced graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into SnO2. The results show that the introduction of graphene quantum dots effectively improved the morphology of the SnO2 film, reduced the roughness of the surface, and increased the ohmic contact between the SnO2 and perovskite layer. In addition, the conductivity of the SnO2 film was improved, thereby facilitating the transfer of electrons. The optimized SnO2: GQDs layer reduced the density of defect state, suppressed the pinholes appearance to a certain extent, and facilitated the extraction of electrons, thereby ultimately improving the FF, and increasing the efficiency of the perovskite photovoltaic device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: SnO2 electron transport
Charge extraction
Solar cell
Graphene quantum dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM topography of SnO2 film with (a) 0%, (b) 1.5%, (c) 3%, (d) 4.5%, (e) 6%GQDs and perovskite film images with (f) 0% (g) 4.5% GQDs.
Conductivity of SnO2 film doped (a) 0%, (b) 1.5%, (c) 3%, (d) 4.5%, (e) 6% GQDs.
XRD patterns of perovskite films grown on different ETLs.
Optical absorption of SnO2 doped GQDs with 0% and 4.5%, Optical absorption of perovskite based on SnO2 doped GQDs with 0%, 4.5% and 6% in the small box.
UPS spectra at the cutoff (Ecutoff) energy regions of SnO2 doped GQDs with (a) 0% and (b) 4.5%. The absorption edge of the SnO2 film doped GQDs with (c) 0% and (d) 4.5%.
Energy band diagram of the SnO2, SnO2: GQDs and perovskite films.
(a) Steady-state photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the FTO/SnO2/perovskite, and FTO/GQDs:SnO2/perovskite, and (b) Time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL).
J–V curves of the best-performing devices based on SnO2 doped with different proportion GQDs.
(a) Distribution of VOC, (b) distribution of JSC, (c) distribution of PCE, (d) distribution of FF, for SnO2 and SnO2:GQDs PSCs.
EQE spectra and the integrated current densities.
Forward and reverse scans J-V of champion devices.
J-V curves of the corresponding PSCs in a dark state.
Mott–Schottky plots of PSCs based on SnO2 and SnO2: GQDs.
(a) Typical photocurrent vs light density; (b) Voc vs light intensity.
SCLC curve of electron-only devices containing the SnO2 film and the GQD-doped SnO2.

(a) Long-term stability of PSCs with ecapsulation based on SnO2 with and without GQDs in under laboratory conditions at 85 °C; (b) Long-term stability of PSCs with ecapsulation based on SnO2 with and without GQDs under laboratory conditions with UV filtered; (c) Long-term stability of PSCs with ecapsulation based on SnO2 with and without GQDs at 85 °C with UV filtered.
Time-resolved PL lifetimes from the biexponential decay model.
Important parameters of J-V curves of the best-performing devices based on SnO2 doped with different proportion GQDs.
Forward and reverse scans J-V of champion devices.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots doping SnO2 for improving carrier transport of perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Condensed Matter and Material Physics, School of Physics, Beihang University, Beijing, 100191, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Condensed Matter and Material Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Condensed Matter and Material Physics, School of Physics, Beihang University, Beijing, 100191, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: SnO2 is recognized as an excellent electron transport layer. However, it does not possess the optimal morphology required by perovskite photovoltaic devices, and the conduction band of SnO2 cannot match well with perovskite conduction bands. In the present study, we introduced graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into SnO2. The results show that the introduction of graphene quantum dots effectively improved the morphology of the SnO2 film, reduced the roughness of the surface, and increased the ohmic contact between the SnO2 and perovskite layer. In addition, the conductivity of the SnO2 film was improved, thereby facilitating the transfer of electrons. The optimized SnO2: GQDs layer reduced the density of defect state, suppressed the pinholes appearance to a certain extent, and facilitated the extraction of electrons, thereby ultimately improving the FF, and increasing the efficiency of the perovskite photovoltaic device.

ce:section.ce:para: Perovskite solar cells were proposed first in 2009 [1,2], and since then have received increasing amounts of attention from scientific research teams due to their various advantages, including low cost, absorption tunability [3,4], mechanical flexibility, and roll-to-roll manufacture capability [5,6]. Perovskite solar cell devices are generally divided into two structures, namely upright (N–I–P) and inverted (P–I–N) structures [7,8]. The upright structure is ITO/ETL/perovskite/HTL/Ag, which is a conventional structure. ITO/HTL/perovskite/ETL/Ag is an upright structure. Among them, the inverted structure is more frequently used in scientific experiments due to its smaller hysteresis, simple preparation process, higher photoelectric conversion efficiency, and higher FF. The conductivity of the electron transport layer (ETL) and matching of energy levels in the cell determine the performance of the perovskite solar cells. High conductivity can improve the extraction and mobility of charges, and reflect the low recombination of defects into which photogenerated carriers fall. Besides, the matching of energy levels facilitates the extraction of charges at the interface, and ultimately improves the photoelectricity conversion rate of the perovskite solar cells. For inorganic oxides, many are widely used. However, compared with inorganic oxides, there are many organic transport layers [9]. Among them, SnO2 and TiO2 arewidely recognized and used [10]. Due to the fact that the preparation of TiO2 requires a rather high temperature and a long period of time, which results in a more complicated heating process, in this study we did not choose TiO2 as the ETL. In contrast, the preparation of SnO2 electron transport layer by SnO2 nanoparticle hydrocolloid requires only a relatively low temperature, and the SnO2 ETL is relatively stable.

However, when SnO2 hydrocolloid is selected as the ETL material, there will be certain defects in it. Due to the fact that the SnO2 ETL prepared by the spin coating process is relatively thin and uneven, it may exhibit pinholes and relatively large roughness on the surface. These defects are not conducive to improving the performance of the perovskite solar cells. Pinhole defects in the transport layer can lead to the decay of perovskite absorption layer, which reduces the performance of perovskite photovoltaic devices [11]. If the surface morphology of the film is poor, it will have a bad effect on the deposition of the upper film, and ultimately not conducive to the performance of the whole perovskite photovoltaic solar cell [12].

In order to resolve the issues described above, researchers have previously optimized the SnO2 electron transport layer from different aspects. For example, Y. Hao et al. improved the electron transport layer by doping different chemical substances: InCl3, polyelectrolyte and LiF. The conductivity of SnO2 and the defects between the passivation electron transport layer and the absorption layer can improve the photoelectric characteristics of perovskite solar cells, and improve the efficiency and stability of perovskite solar cells [13–15]. Tingli Ma added lanthanide elements in the electron transport layer can improve the photoelectric conversion efficiency of perovskite photovoltaic solar cells, which is conducive to the improvement of the performance of photovoltaic solar cells [16]. In addition, Xiaoliang Zhang used passivation materials to passivate the surface of SnO2 [17]. Red carbon quantum dots are used by Yonghua Chen et al. to study perovskite photovoltaic solar cells [18]. With the rise of quantum dots, Li Gang and others considered to apply them in flexible large area photovoltaic solar cells [19]. Rare earth elements have attracted the attention of Shengzhong Frank Liu et al., and it is used to improve the photoelectric performance of perovskite photovoltaic solar cells [20]. Thomas M. Brown et al. Fabricated Perovskite photovoltaic solar cells with double transport layers [21].Y. Hao et al. deposited a glycine buffer layer between the SnO2/perovskite interface to passivate the interface defects, reduce the recombination of electrons and holes, and increase the stability of perovskite photovoltaic devices [22].

In the present paper, we introduced graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into SnO2 hydrocolloid, to form a dense electron transport layer by means of a spinning process. The study results show that the introduction of graphene quantum dots has improved the photoelectric conversion efficiency of perovskite photovoltaic devices. It can also be see that the doping of graphene quantum dots into SnO2 reduces the surface roughness of the electron transport layer, thereby reducing the interfacial defects in perovskite devices. In addition, the conductivity of the electron transport layer is improved, and the square resistance is reduced. At the same time, the conduction band position of SnO2 is adjusted, which is beneficial to the increase of open circuit voltage. From the results, we determine that the optimized FF is 78%, PCE is 18.55%, and VOC is 1.10 V.

DMF, DMSO,CB, Li-TFSI, tBP and CAN were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich; PbI2 FAI, MABr, PbBr2, Spiro-OMeTAD purchased from Xi'an Polymer Light, 4-tert-butylpyridine Graphene quantum dots (GQDs, 1 mg/mL) purchased from Suzhou Tanfeng Graphene Technology Co., Ltd.); SnCl2·2H2O and SnO2 purchased from Alfa Aesar, gold electrode purchased from Zhongnuo Advanced Material Technology Co., Ltd.

We took one-step process. 1 M of FAI, 1.1 M PbI2, 0.2 M MABr and 0.22 M PbBr2 to 1 mL of DMF and DMSO (4:1) mixed solvent. The precursor solution was placed on a magnetic stirrer in a glove box. The magnetic stirrer temperature was set at 60.72.3 mg of Spiro Spiro-OMeTAD, 1 mL of CB, 17.5 mL of Li-TFSI solution and 28.8 L of tBP mixed. Filter the prepared solution in turn using a 0.44 μm filter after stirring for 12 h. The ETM SnO2 solution was prepared by doped different quality rate GQDs (GQDs: SnO2 = 0%, 1.5%, 3%, 4.5%, 6%).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the perovskite solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: Conductive glass cleaning process used acetone, ethyl alcohol and deionized water, respectively. The SnO2 hydrocolloid with different doping quality rate GQDs (SnO2 = 0%, 1.5%, 3%, 4.5%, 6%) was deposited on FTO glass at 3000 rpm for 30 s. The FTO glass was then annealed for 1 h to form the ETL of the cells. Afterwards, the perovskite precursor solutions were spin-coated on the ETL at 6000 r/min for 30 s to form the absorber layers of the solar cells. Finally, the HTL Spiro-OMeTAD solution was spin-coated on the perovskite films at 4000 r/min. The silver electrode of photovoltaic device was evaporated by tungsten wire thermal evaporation equipment in vacuum.

The Hitachi S-4800 cold field scanning electron microscopy (SEM) system was used to test crystal morphology. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) were tested using a Bruker D8 X ray diffractometer. The absorption spectra tested with a Rayleigh UV-1601 spectro-photometer. The photoluminescence spectra (PL) was recorded using an Edinburgh Instrument FLS920. The relationship between JSC and VOC of perovskite photovoltaic solar cells were measured using a CROWNTECH Semiconductor parameter analysis instrument. The EQE were tested with a SOFN 7-SCSpec solar cell measurements system in AC mode.

SEM images of SnO2 films with different contents of graphene quantum dots are shown in Fig. 1 . It can be seen from the figures that in the pristine SnO2 film, several pinholes appear on the surface of the film. This is due to the fact that the thickness of the SnO2 film is relatively thin, while, at the same time, the roughness of the SnO2 film is relatively large. The appearance of pinholes will result in direct contact between the FTO electrode and absorber films, forming a large number of defects, in turn causing charges the recombination of charges in the surface of SnO2. In addition, the thin SnO2 films cannot form an ohmic contact with perovskite films. However, when the doping concentration of graphene quantum dots is less than 4.5%, it can be found that the surface morphology of SnO2 thin films gradually becomes conducive to the crystallization of the upper perovskite thin films. That is to say, holes and roughness gradually decrease.When the concentration of graphene quantum dots in the SnO2 hydrocolloid reaches 4.5%, the morphology of SnO2 thin film is favorable to form ohmic contact with the absorption layer, which is beneficial to improve the photoelectric properties of perovskite. However, when the concentration of graphene quantum dots in SnO2 hydrocolloid exceeds 4.5%, then it can be seen from the figure that, as the concentration of the GQDs increases, the GQDs will gradually accumulate on the surface of the SnO2 film, resulting in the roughness of the SnO2 film increasing in size. As is well known, the smaller roughness of SnO2 surface is, the flatter the surface of perovskite film will be, which is beneficial to improving the performance of perovskite photovoltaic devices. It can be observed from the SEM image that, when the concentration of GQDs in the SnO2 hydrocolloid is 4.5%, this is available for growing the perovskite films.

Next, we tested the SEM of perovskite films grown on the surface of the SnO2 film doped with graphene quantum dots of 0% and 4.5%. . From Fig. 1(f) and Figs. 1(g) that the perovskite grain on SnO2 doped with 4.5% graphene quantum dots is larger than that grown on the surface of pristine SnO2.The small white particles shown in the images are caused by excessive PbI2 in the precursor solution. Lead iodide remains in the perovskite absorption layer, which is conducive to the stability of perovskite crystal, the charge transfer in the absorption layer and the improvement of perovskite properties [23]. From Fig. 1(f) and Figs. 1(g), it can be concluded that the morphology of SnO2 has a certain effect on the crystallization of the perovskite film.

Using the four-probe resistivity/square resistance tester, we tested the square resistance of the SnO2 films doped graphene quantum dots with doping concentrations of 0%, 1.5%, 3%, 4.5%, and 6%, and the results were 54 Ω/□, 44 Ω/□, 47 Ω/□, 35 Ω/□ and 62 Ω/□, respectively. We can then determine the corresponding resistivity through the relationship among the square resistance, resistivity and conductivity, using the following equations (Eqn 1 and 2):
(1)
R =
ρ d
(2)
σ =
1 ρ
where
R
represents square resistance,
ρ
represents electrical resistivity, and
d
represents film thickness. The film thickness used in this study is 50 nm, and
σ
represents electrical conductivity. On the basis of Eqs. (1) and (2), we then determine the conductivity of the five samples, which are respectively shown to be 3.7 × 105 S/m, 4.5 × 105 S/m, 4.3 × 105 S/m, 5.7 × 105 S/m and 3.2 × 105 S/m, (Fig. 2 ) when the content of graphene quantum dots in SnO2 increases gradually, which leads to an increase in conductivity. This is due to the fact that graphene quantum dots are doped in SnO2, thereby reducing the roughness of the SnO2 film, and compensating for the defects of the SnO2 film. When SnO2 contain 4.5% GQDs, then the maximum conductivity is 5.7 × 105 s/m. However, when the content of graphene quantum dots in SnO2 films exceeds 4.5%, the electrical properties of SnO2 films decrease gradually. With the increase of the content of graphene quantum dots in SnO2 films, the graphene quantum dots accumulate excessively, which worsens the surface morphology of SnO2 films [24], after which a large number of electrons are captured, thereby reducing the conductivity of the SnO2 film.

According to the equivalent circuit of perovskite solar cells, the output current of the equivalent circuit can be reduced to (Eqn 3):
(3)
I =
I ph
−
I 0
(
e
q
(
V + I R
)
n k T
− 1
)
−
V + I R
R
s h

Iph is photogenerated current. When I = 0 in the formula, the output current is 0, and the series resistance is 0, which is only related to the parallel resistance. The change trend of shunt resistance and open circuit voltage is approximately the same. When I ≠ 0, the effect of series resistance on fill factor is obvious. In addition, the larger the series resistance is, the more obvious the decrease of short circuit current is. In practice, the series resistance is obtained by calculating the slope at the open circuit voltage of the I–V curve [25].

Square resistance and series resistance, parallel resistance have positive correlation and negative correlation respectively. When the RSH is large, this signifies that the leakage current of the device is relatively small. Additionally, a low RS indicates that the electrode and absorption layer form an ohmic contact, while the contact resistance between the interfaces is relatively small. Therefore, a smaller square resistance of the SnO2 film leads to a favorable ohmic contact at the interface, thereby increasing the collection of carriers at the electrode and reducing the leakage current in the cells.

From Fig. 3 , the two vertical XRD patterns correspond the perovskite films grown on the surface of SnO2 films which were undoped and doped with 4.5% graphene quantum dots. The existing (001) PbI2 peaks are uniformly located at the 12.7° position, while (110), (220) and (310) are located at the corresponding positions, and their peak heights are somewhat different. The XRD peak value of perovskite film based on the SnO2 doped with graphene quantum dots with a concentration of 4.5% is higher than the XRD peak value of the perovskite film grown on the surface of the undoped SnO2 film. Therefore, the XRD position of the upper perovskite crystal remains unchanged and the crystal orientation changes little before and after SnO2 is optimized.It further shows that the upper perovskite absorption layer has little effect on the light absorption before and after SnO2 optimization.There is a (001) peak in the XRD, thus indicating that there is excess unreacted PbI2 present, which is Mutual verification with SEM. The residual PbI2 can improve the performance of perovskite devices to a certain extent [26]. It can be seen from the XRD results that the SnO2 film doped with graphene quantum dots of different concentrations has a certain effect on the perovskite crystallization, which improves the crystal quality of perovskite films, and increases the flatness. This observation is consistent with the previous SEM images of perovskites grown on different substrates.

Fig. 4 shows the UV absorption spectra of SnO2 films doped with different graphene quantum dots amounts. From the figure, the SnO2 film doped with graphene quantum dots at a concentration of 4.5% absorption intensity has a significant improvement.

In the small box in the upper right corner of Fig. 4 shows the optical absorption of perovskite based on SnO2 doped GQDs with 0%, 4.5% and 6%. The absorption of the perovskite film based on SnO2 doped with 4.5% GQDs has a smaller absorption edges, while that based on 6% GQDs get a bigger one. This is due to the fact that, when the doping concentration is 4.5%, then the surface of the SnO2 film is relatively flat, which is conducive to the crystallization of the perovskite film. When doped with 6% GQDs, the surface roughness of the SnO2 film is very poor, which causes the crystal sequence of the perovskite film to be disordered. This conclusion is consistent with the SEM image of the perovskite film.
Next, we measured the UPS and absorption spectrum of the SnO2 and SnO2: GQDs films, then obtained the energy band structure diagram of the perovskite solar cells, as shown in Fig. 5 and.Fig. 6 .

According to the measured UPS spectrum and absorption spectrum of the SnO2 films and SnO2: GQDs films, as shown in Fig. 5, we obtain the Ecut off value before and after optimization from Fig. 5(a) and (b), as well as the Eedge value before and after optimization from the UPS spectrum shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d), based on the following equation (Eqn 4):
(4)
E F
=
E
cu t o f f
− 21.22 e V

Next, the relative Fermi level can be calculated; 21.22 eV is a set number [27], which represents an excitation energy when measuring UPS. Next get it by (Eqn 5):
(5)
E V
=
E F
−
E edge
Using the obtained Fermi energy level, subtracting the Eedge value obtained from Fig. 5, we can get the corresponding EV valence band energy level.

The valence band value of the SnO2 sample optimized and not optimized by the graphene quantum dots is 8.01 eV and 7.62 eV. In addition, we measured the SnO2 absorption spectra, and observed that these absorption spectra of the SnO2 films were almost identical. This indicated that SnO2 does not react with graphene quantum dots to form new materials.Therefore, the absorption edges and calculated band gaps are also the same, and thus we determined that the conduction bands of the SnO2 sample optimized by the GQDs and of those not optimized are 3.92 eV and 4.31 eV, respectively. The reason for this is that the Fermi level of doped SnO2 film changes, which affects the change of the conduction band position, and ultimately affects the VOC of perovskite photovoltaic solar cells (Fig. 6).

As shown in Fig. 7 (a), in order to test and compare the influence of GQDs in SnO2, we measured the corresponding PL spectrum. From the PL spectrum, we observed that the intensity of the PL curve of the sample based on SnO2 film doped graphene quantum dot with 4.5% concentration is significantly lower than that of the PL curve of the sample based on SnO2 film doped graphene quantum dots with 0% concentration. The SnO2 electron transport layer with graphene quantum dots can improve the surface morphology, knowledge electron transport layer and upper layer perovskite absorption layer is formed to form ohmic contact, so as to improve the photoelectric characteristics of photovoltaic solar cells [28].

Fig. 7(b) shows the TRPL patterns of a perovskite photovoltaic device based on SnO2 film that is not doped with graphene quantum dots and a perovskite photovoltaic device with SnO2 film doped graphene quantum dots at a concentration of 4.5%. The TRPL decay time is fitted with the following quadratic equation (Eq. (6)) [29]. In the equation, τ1 represents the fast decay component, τ2 represents the slow decay component, and A1 and A2 represent the amplitude, respectively. The average decay time is determined following the equation (Eqn 6):
(6)
τ
a v e
=
(
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
)
/
(
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
)
The decay time is shown in Table 1 :

It is calculated that (Table 1) the average decay time of photovoltaic devices based on SnO2 doped with graphene quantum dots of 0% and 4.5% are 125.91 ns and 92.00 ns, respectively. It can be concluded that, when the SnO2 is optimized by graphene quantum dots, the defects on the surface of the SnO2 are reduced, the corrosion of the electrode to the perovskite absorption layer is reduced, and the ability of the electron transport layer to extract charges in the absorption layer is improved. Consequently, the transmission speed is increased and the time is shortened, thereby reducing the recombination of the charge at the interface.

From Fig. 9 (a), the average VOC of the perovskite photovoltaic solar cells based on the SnO2: GQDs is 1.095 V, which is to a certain extent higher than the VOC 1.06 V of the perovskite photovoltaic solar cells based on the SnO2 not optimized by GQDs. This is due to the fact that the energy levels of the optimized SnO2 electron transport layer and perovskite layer are more closely matched, which improves the transmission and extraction of electrons. The VOC and JSC distribution diagrams of 20 photovoltaic solar cells before and after optimization are shown in Fig. 9(a) and (b). The average value (FF) of the optimized solar cells is higher, as shown in Fig. 9(d). Fig. 9(c) shows that the PCE of the perovskite solar cells has improved significantly. From Fig. 9(a), (b), (c) and (d), it can be seen that the optimized data are relatively uniform, while those of the unoptimized photovoltaic devices are relatively divergent. This shows that the optimized photovoltaic device has higher repeatability and rather stable performance.

In the following analysis, we select the most favorable characteristics of the perovskite solar cell devices based on SnO2 film doped with GQDs, the concentrations of which are 4.5% and 0%. The EQE spectrum of the perovskite solar cell based on SnO2 thin film and the corresponding JSC obtained by integration are shown in Fig. 10 . The graph corresponds to the spectrum in the range of 350–800 nm. The trends of the two EQE curves in the 350–800 nm are almost identical, but the respective intensities are different, while the EQE of the photovoltaic devices based on SnO2 thin film doped with graphene quantum dots of 4.5% is higher than that based on doped graphene quantum dots with a concentration of 0% SnO2 thin film. This is due to the fact that the optimized SnO2 film flattens, the roughness is reduced, the defects and recombination are reduced, and ohmic contact is formed between the electron transport layer and absorption layer, thereby increasing the crystallinity of the perovskite film, and the electrical properties of the corresponding perovskite photovoltaic solar cells. The corresponding integrated JSC from the EQE spectra is observed to be consistent with the short-circuit current value from the J-V curves as shown in Fig. 11 .

The following section compares the J-V curve hysteresis of perovskite photovoltaic cells based on the optimized SnO2 with that based on pristine SnO2 film that had not been optimized. The samples of the two groups were tested under the same conditions, scanning forward and reverse. Upon comparison (Table 3 ), it is apparent that the hysteresis of the photovoltaic device based on the SnO2 thin film modified with GQDs is smaller than that of the photovoltaic device without optimized SnO2.

The calculation of hysteresis generally uses the following equation (Eqn 7) [30]:
(7)
H I =
(
η
R everse
−
η
F o r w o r d
)
η
Re v e r s e

The electrical performance parameters of the optimized photovoltaic device and the unoptimized photovoltaic devices are shown in Table 1. It can be seen from the table that, for optimized samples, the ohmic contact between the SnO2 film and perovskite absorption layer is increased, the extraction and transfer of charges are improved, and the recombination of defects is reduced, so that the VOC and photoelectric conversion efficiency (PCE) of the photovoltaic solar cells are increased. Consequently, the corresponding hysteresis is reduced.

Fig. 12 indicates the relationship between short circuit current and open circuit voltage without illumination. The dark-state current density of the optimized perovskite solar cells is lower than that of the pristine cells, while the lower dark-state current leads to a higher open circuit voltage. This is due to the improvement of electrical properties and morphology of SnO2 films doped with GQDs. [31], and the ohmic contact is finally improved, thereby achieving an increase in Voc and FF.

The C-2-V curves of the photovoltaic solar cells based on SnO2 optimized by graphene quantum dots and non-optimized perovskite photovoltaic devices are shown in Fig. 13. From Fig. 14 , the slope of the treated PV device increases, which means that the defect state concentration in the PV devices decreases, so as to improve the performance of the perovskite PV solar cells [32].

Fig. 14 shows the relationship of short-circuit current density and open-circuit voltage are dependent on light intensity. The graphs respectively show the short-circuit current density and open-circuit voltage of photovoltaic solar cells based on SnO2 thin film doped with 4.5% graphene quantum dots and that of photovoltaic devices based on the initial SnO2 thin film as a function of light intensity. As shown in Fig. 14(a), with the increase of light intensity, the slope of the short-circuit current based on photovoltaic devices with optimized SnO2 film is higher than that based on the pristine SnO2. This shows that, in the optimized SnO2 film sample, the defects are reduced, the charge recombination is reduced, and the performance of the perovskite photovoltaic device is increased [33,34]. As illustrated in Fig. 14(b), with the increase of light intensity, the open circuit voltage slope of photovoltaic devices based on optimized SnO2 film is lower than that based on the pristine SnO2 film. This reveals that the defects of the SnO2 film in the electron transport layer are reduced, and the recombination of charges in the electron transport layer and the interface is reduced, thereby improving the charge extraction ability. At last, the parameters of photovoltaic solar cells have been improved [35].

Fig. 15 shows the space-charge limited current (SCLC) curve of a perovskite photovoltaic solar cell containing only an electron transport layer, and the structure is FTO/SnO2/CsFAMA perovskite/spiro/Ag. One of the curves is based on SnO2 film optimized by graphene quantum dots, while the other is based on SnO2 film that has not been optimized. Through this test of SCLC, we can determine the defects of the SnO2 electron transport layer. The curve is divided into three areas, namely the ohmic area, space-charge limited current area, and defect-filled area [36,37]. The defect state Nt of the films can be calculated by the following equation (Eqn 8) [38]:
(8)
N t
=
2 ε
ε 0
V
T F L
e
L 2
where e represents the element charge, L represents the thickness of the absorption layer, and ε and ε 0 represent the relative permittivity and vacuum permittivity of the perovskite absorption layer, respectively. Based on the SCLC curve, we can determine the trap-filled limit voltage (VTFL). Finally, the Nt was 7.68 × 1015 cm-3, based on the SnO2 thin film optimized by graphene quantum dots and 10.71 × 1015 cm-3, based on the non-optimized SnO2 thin film solar cells.

Through the obtained Nt value we can determine that the defect state density of the SnO2 sample optimized by graphene quantum dots is significantly reduced, indicating that the GQDs exert a positive influence on the SnO2 film. The interface contact between the absorbing layers and SnO2 improves, and the roughness of the SnO2 surface decreases, which is consistent with the previous test. Therefore, the optimization of graphene quantum dots reduces the defects of SnO2, as well as the recombination of interface electrons and holes.

The stability of perovskite solar cells is an important aspect that requires our attention. In the this paper, in order to study the stability, we consider the stability of optimized and unoptimized perovskite photovoltaic solar cells from three aspects (Fig. 16). First, as shown in Fig. 16 (a), the ecapsulation simples under the conditions of laboratory at 85 °C, the obtained samples was continuously irradiated with one-sun illumination with UV filtered. The efficiency of the perovskite photovoltaic device optimized by graphene quantum dots was approximately remain 90% after irradiating at 85°Cfor 250 h with UV filtered, while that of the unoptimized perovskite photovoltaic solar cells was about 72% remained. Second, as shown in Fig. 16(b), we placed the optimized and non-optimized photovoltaic solar cells with ecapsulation under laboratory conditions at the temperature of 25 °C and continuous sunlight with UV for 250 h, to measure the efficiency of the solar cells. It was observed that the efficiency of the device optimized by graphene quantum dots was 92% of the pristine efficiency, yet the efficiency of the unoptimized photovoltaic device was about 70% of the initial efficiency. Finally, as shown in Fig. 16(c), the optimized and unoptimized perovskite photovoltaic solar cells with ecapsulation were placed in the bad environment (temperature 80 °C, with UV) under continuous light for 250 h, and it was shown that the efficiency of the optimized photovoltaic device was 85% of the initial efficiency, yet the efficiency of the non-optimized photovoltaic solar cells was only about 55% of the initial efficiency. Through these measurements, we discovered that the stability of photovoltaic solar cells with ecapsulation optimized by GQDs is greater than that of the pristine perovskite photovoltaic solar cells. This is due to the fact that the SnO2 electron transport layer optimized by GQDs reduces surface defects, as well as the recombination of electrons and holes between the perovskite layer and electron transport layer, thereby decelerating the attenuation of performances of the perovskite photovoltaic solar cells, and enhancing the stability of perovskite photovoltaic solar cells.

In summary, in this study we doped an appropriate amount of GQDs in the SnO2 electron transport layer. The experiment proved that the morphology of the optimized SnO2 electron transport layer was improved, the energy levels of the electron transport layer and perovskite absorption layer matched well, and the conductivity was increased, thereby effectively improving the conversion efficiency of the perovskite photovoltaic solar cells. The photoelectric conversion efficiency of the perovskite photovoltaic solar cells based on the optimized SnO2 electron transport layer was as high as 18.55%.The optimized electron transport layer effectively reduced the interface defects caused by the rough interface and recombination of electrons and holes caused by the interface defects, and also improved the stability of the perovskite photovoltaic solar cells. This paper describes the doping of an appropriate amount of graphene quantum dots in the SnO2 layer as a simple method by which to effectively improve the efficiency of perovskite photovoltaic devices and improve the stability of photovoltaic solar cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge Beihang University for providing access to experimental equipment to be used for samples fabrication and measurement. The project was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (grant no. 51572008).
